criteria: Published by a recognized news organization or official public agency
Connected to at least one Family PD pillar
Offers a practical prevention, preparation, or family-learning opportunity

stories.title: Sudden severe storm turns a holiday boat trip into a tragedy
stories.summary: A sudden severe storm overwhelmed a recreational boat on Geneva Lake. Three children died even though the children were wearing life jackets, showing how quickly weather conditions can exceed a family’s ability to react.
prevention: Check forecasts and active alerts immediately before outdoor or water activities.
Decide in advance which weather conditions mean the activity stops or the family returns to shore.
Use life jackets, but do not treat safety equipment as a replacement for weather decisions and evacuation timing.
Create a communication and reunification plan for outings.
stories.prompts: What warning signs or weather alerts would make our family cancel or leave an activity? Who checks the forecast? What is our emergency communication and meeting plan during an outing?

stories.title: Making a family plan for summer screen time
stories.summary: The FTC encouraged families to discuss online rules and expectations and consider parental controls for content, time limits, communication, purchases, location sharing, and separate device profiles.
prevention: Agree on screen-time expectations before conflict occurs.
Review who children can communicate with through games and social platforms.
Use device profiles, passcodes, privacy settings, and age-appropriate parental controls.
Discuss how online activity affects sleep, mood, school, safety, and family relationships.
stories.prompts: What are our current screen-time expectations? Which apps, games, chats, purchases, or location settings need review? What should children do when an unknown person contacts them?

stories.title: What families should know after the Canvas cyberattack
stories.summary: The FTC warned that after a school technology breach, scammers may impersonate schools or companies. Families were advised not to trust links or contact information in unexpected messages and to use known school or company channels.
prevention: Do not click links or call numbers in unexpected breach-related messages.
Contact the school or company through a known website, phone number, or email address.
Review account passwords and use multifactor authentication where available.
Know where to find identity-theft and data-breach guidance before it is needed.
stories.prompts: How would we verify a message claiming our school account or personal information was exposed? Which family accounts need stronger passwords or multifactor authentication? Who should children ask before responding?

stories.title: That unexpected job-offer text is probably a scam
stories.summary: The FTC reported fake recruiters sending generic texts about remote jobs, then asking people to send money, deposit fake checks, or put personal money into online tasks.
prevention: Do not engage with generic, unexpected job texts or messaging-app offers.
Never pay money to get paid or secure a job.
Verify employers through the organization’s official website and published contact information.
Ask a trusted adult or experienced job seeker to review unfamiliar offers.
stories.prompts: What signs make a job offer suspicious? How will our family verify recruiters and employers? What information or money should never be provided during an unexpected job conversation?
